1: shadow Make haste, O God, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me! The urgent petition — the eschatological cry of the church awaiting Christ's return: Come, Lord Jesus! (Revelation 22:20). Christ inhabits this urgency in Gethsemane where the hour of trial comes upon him; the church prays it in his name, longing for the deliverer to hasten his coming.
2: shadow Let them be put to shame and confusion who seek my life! Let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt! The shaming of those who sought Christ's life — overturned at the resurrection. Those who delighted in his hurt at the cross are confounded by the empty tomb: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (Acts 2:24), bringing dishonor on every scheme devised against the righteous one.
3: shadow Let them turn back because of their shame who say, Aha, Aha! The Aha! of mockery at the cross (Mark 15:29 — the crowd wagging their heads and saying Aha!) is the specific taunt turned back on the mockers at the resurrection. The shame they intended for Christ rebounds on those who said it, as they cannot deny the empty tomb (Matthew 28:11-15).
4: shadow May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you! May those who love your salvation say evermore, God is great! The joy of those who seek God — the disciples' joy at the resurrection (John 20:20 — the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord). Those who love his salvation say evermore God is great — the church's doxology grounded in the resurrection, the definitive display of God's greatness.
5: shadow But I am poor and needy; hasten to me, O God! You are my help and my deliverer; O LORD, do not delay! Christ is the poor and needy one — though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9). From this poverty he cries for the Father to hasten, as in Gethsemane (if it be possible, let this cup pass, Matthew 26:39). The Father hastens in resurrection; the church prays the same cry for his parousia.
